title: Bearers of Innovation: Keynote by Prof. Robert S. Langer
text:
it's it's a pleasure for me to be able
to speak uh to you on uh inventor day I
thought I would just tell you a little
bit about uh myself and some of the
things that I've done you know I I had
the great pleasure of being at Cornell
uh from 1966 to 1970 and then later on
uh being on the Board of Trustees and
even had two of my children uh go to
Cornell after Cornell I went to MIT um
and uh and and when I finished I began
working in a hospital and if we could go
onto the first next Slide the project I
began working on was to see if we could
discover molecules that could stop blood
vessels from growing to tumors and and
this is just a picture of of why that's
important because when blood vessels
grow to the tumor uh they enable the
tumor to get much much bigger and if you
go to the upper right actually spread
through those blood vessels that's a
process called metastasis which can can
kill people uh when I started working
with uh my adviser Judah folman there
had been really was no way to study
blood vessel growth so we had to
actually invent a way to do it and if we
could go to the next
slide what we did is there's certain
places in the body that uh like the the
eye the Rabbid eye we used also the
chick corol and to membrane that have no
blood vessels or very few and so our
idea to study this was to put a tumor
there and then a tiny little particle
like a nano particle or microparticle
that could deliver the large molecules
that we were trying to isolate no one
had ever done that before and the
scientific literature and scientists in
general would say what's on the next
slide that if you tried to put a large
molecule in a tiny particle like it was
encased in that tiny particle it just
couldn't get out it was just too big
like walking through brick wall that was
the conventional wisdom at the
time um but uh if we were going to solve
the blood vessel problem uh we had to
solve this problem so I spent two years
working in the laboratory experimenting
with different techniques and I found
over 200 different ways to get this to
not work but eventually on the next
slide we were able to make tiny little
particles like you see a picture of one
on the left and cut in half on the right
and we showed uh in the journal Nature
uh next slide
that you could use this for the very
first time to release all kinds of
proteins and even nucleic acids this was
the first paper ever showing you could
make take take tiny particles uh and
deliver nucleic acids it's a 1976 paper
nonetheless when I presented this work
again it met with a lot of skepticism
some of that's shown in the next slide
and people would criticize the work and
say they couldn't believe it because of
the large molecules couldn't get through
the materials and they also said the
solvents I was using um like methylene
chloride and and ethanol would destroy
whatever we put in but we kept
persisting um um uh I I had done a lot
of this work at Boston Children's
Hospital with Judah fulman who I was my
adviser and then I would continue it at
MIT now because we had in spite of that
skepticism uh you know I or maybe
because of it uh you know we just kept
going you know my first nine grants were
rejected I couldn't get a job uh in in a
um in a chemical engineering department
actually I ended up in a Nutrition
department for a while but because we
had this approach of having the the
nanop particles or microparticles and
and we developed the bioassay we could
study for the first time whether we
could stop tumors from growing and I'm
going to show you a bloody slide but
there's no other way to show it so we
published a paper in science in 1976
next slide and it showed for the first
time that you could stop blood vessels
from growing uh what what happens and
normally in hundreds of fractions we
tested uh on the left and they all had
these sheets of blood vessels growing
over them but one of them did not we
repeated the one on the right 20 times
and notied the giant difference in in in
vascularization um and that would
ultimately lead these techniques many
years later because of great work at
genitech and other companies to uh
what's on the next slide which is a
summary of all kinds of uh angiogenesis
Inhibitors blood vessel Inhibitors uh
aasin for example is the number two or
three bestselling biotech drug in
history and there are many of these
drugs that are now approved and these
are really the only ways to find
molecules drugs like IIA and Luc centus
that can prevent diseases of blindness
in the back of the eye like macur
generation
now going back to the 70s Dr folman and
I we wanted to get a patent Children's
Hospital never had one before we filed
for one and uh five years in a row it
got rejected and everyone told me to
give up but I told you in the beginning
that when we first did this everyone
said it was impossible it could never
work and I wondered maybe somebody wrote
that down so after five years of
rejection I started to I did what's
called a a science citation search
looking back at our 1976 nature paper to
see who wrote anything about what we
said so I I found actually some
interesting things and one of the
articles I found next slide uh stated
the following describing this very young
field I'll just read some of it to you
they said the agent to be released is a
relatively small molecule with a
molecular weight no larger than a few
hundred one wouldn't expect that macro
molecules for example proteins could uh
be released because of such a technique
because of their extremely small per
peration rates however folkman and
myself have uh reported some surprising
results that clearly um demonstrate the
opposite so uh I showed that to the
people at the hospital and they uh
showed it to the patent examiner and he
said GE he had no idea but he said he
will allow this patent if I could get
written affidavits from each of these
pat p scientists on the bottom that they
really wrote that so I was a young
Professor I wrote them back and uh they
uh actually uh said that they did write
that and on the next slide we got this
very broad patent a first time on
controlled release of macromolecules and
we used that and other things we did
next slide to uh and and I got involved
in starting companies and teaching
companies and it would lead to all kinds
of new products we worked with teada for
example and taught them how to make
these and they came up with what's
called Lupron Depot which is a product
that's still widely used today uh to to
deliver a peptide to treat Advanced
prostate cancer and endometriosis I
think and and and and many others you
see up here like risad Al cona which is
a way that many millions of people with
schizophrenia would get treated um and
Vivitrol which is a once a month
treatment for opioid addiction and many
many others that you see here and not
only has it been useful in medicine it
really helped because of Yanni Zohar one
of our visiting scientists and now
professor in Baltimore uh this whole
area of aquaculture you see in the
bottom right because for the first time
you could uh you know control spawning
and and uh even preserve rare fish
now one of the other things that this
would lead to uh many years later if I
could go to the next slide was the idea
that you could not only deliver U large
molecules and like proteins and peptides
and protect them but maybe you could
deliver messenger
RNA so the idea as you probably know is
DNA makes RNA makes protein by
2010 uh people uh had 70% of the top
bestselling drugs were proteins but and
people had done work on on RNA there was
a a famous paper back in uh nature in
1990 showing that that you might be able
to get RNA uh to to make proteins in in
animals but AR but people would find
that RNA you know might cause different
negative effects but there was an
important paper by kico and Weissman who
won the Nobel Prize last year in
medicine for this work showing that if
you modified uh the messenger RNA
chemically in certain ways those effects
would go away they actually wrote a
patent and they tried to start a company
but nobody would give them funding
because they didn't think this was
possible a few years later Derek Rossy
who was a professor at Harvard actually
began to use these Tech techniques to
create IPS cells this is a certain like
stem light type of cell um and and he
also was interested in starting a
company he did this with messenger RNA
and so he tried to get funding and went
to some very good places like Third Rock
and again uh they told him this you know
could never work and then then he came
to see me and I thought well actually I
I think we can get it to work largely
because of the these inventions we had
made not only the the idea shown in the
nature paper that you could deliver
large molecules but we had actually
taken it further uh we'd come up with
ways to coat it uh with that with
polyethylene glycol a paper we wrote in
science with reandra graph we' come up
with ways to uh create really I what are
called ionizable um parts of lipids or
or polymers actually this work was done
by Dan pack and who's now professor in
in Kentucky and and actually David putam
who's a professor at Cornell in the year
2000 and again in
2001 uh and and so but then to even take
these ionizable materials you you want
to have a lot of them and Dan Anderson
one of our our our postto now professor
at MIT and David Lynn came up with ways
to make libraries and then we even came
up with ways that roer carick in our lab
now professor at MIT did by using
microfluidic procedures to make these
and I thought that was scalable so my
feeling was that we could certainly make
messenger RNA Therapeutics by putting
them in the right kind of nanoparticles
so in so with Derek and I talked to
Newar fean who's a MIT graduate and
started Flagship Ventures about starting
a company and we started this company
madna uh Ken Chen was also involved uh
who was a cardiologist and and and and
that was really the idea that you could
have messenger RNA Therapeutics then
next slide show just shows that you can
actually treat diseases you could never
treat before like make use mRNA to make
new intracellular or membrane bound
proteins and the next slide really shows
something that's been kind of historic
that when covid came even though people
didn't think madna could do these things
really in two days um after the Chinese
scientists published the virus genetic
sequence they they designed the vaccine
then put it in the nanop particles
shipped it to NIH for testing and the
first patients were treated uh on March
16th and this uh got approved even for
uh for use uh all over the world and the
next slide just shows a picture of of
the vial and the next slide uh just
shows some of the data um that it was
94% effective just for reference flu is
about 40% affected and the only two
vaccines in the United United States
that would get approved are uh were met
messenger RNA in nanop particles using
some of the principles that we developed
the next slide just shows really again
why you want to get
a vaccinated makes an enormous
difference in terms of hospitalizations
and the next slide just shows that this
is not an easy thing uh this was uh
really in less than a 2-year period
there were 49 vaccines that failed and
only two approved there were 209
treatments that different companies
tried to make and only one approved and
with antivirals 153 failed and there was
only one approved um nature uh would
write um that uh different people would
write if we could go to the next slide
that um that really this 1976 invention
and of course as I mentioned continued
by many great people in in our
laboratory over the years including
David putam you know would really uh
raise the possibility of making this as
a drug and not only can it be used to um
to to you know for Co and other V other
regular vaccines but what's really
exciting is it may open the door to
cancer vaccines and that's going to be
shown on the last slide that I want to
go over which is uh really what madna
and MK are doing is using this to treat
patients with melanoma here you see 157
patients with melanoma uh and what we're
doing is making a personalized cancer
vaccine that's done by selecting up to
34 mutations known as neoepitopes in the
patient's cancer cell and you take their
genetic code and you do exactly what you
did for Co you know you just incorporate
that genetic code into the MRNA uh in
nanoparticles in the trial uh half of
the patients and it's again stubble
blind uh get Merks drug guda which is
probably the best immunotherapy drug and
half guda plus the personalized cancer
vaccine and what you see is at 2 years
uh the risk of recurrence or death was
reduced by 44% and at three years
reduced by 49% so I I want to end here
but just make a few comments that to me
invention has been a wonderful thing you
know it's very interesting when I look
at what's happened um with with even the
whole area of mRNA it's just too little
companies madna and and bio inch that
took the risk because of of of the
different work that people did uh and
and and made messenger RNA Therapeutics
which really I think changed the world
uh by by these vaccines and I think
we'll continue to do so no large company
would get involved until fizer finally
in 2020 uh you know started working with
uh with bio entech no one was willing to
work with us uh which was probably ended
up for the better in the sense of of
what what's happened with madna but so
much of this has had to do with
invention uh with patents and and uh I I
just hope you have a wonderful uh day
today you know at this uh invention
celebration and I know from my
experience at Cornell the wonderful work
that everybody does thank you so much
